prompt: A 110 V source powers three resistors in series: 10 Ω, 15 Ω, and 30 Ω. What is the total current in amps?
hint: First add up all the resistances, then use Ohm's Law: I = V / R.

solution.summary: In a series circuit, resistances add together. Then apply Ohm's Law to find the current.

steps.title: Identify the circuit type
steps.body: All three resistors are in a single loop, so this is a series circuit.

steps.title: Choose the formula
steps.body: Ohm's Law tells us current equals voltage divided by resistance.

steps.title: Interpret the result
steps.body: In a series circuit, the same 2 A of current flows through every component.
steps.note: If any one resistor were removed, the circuit would break and no current would flow.

solution.whyItWorks: Ohm's Law (I = V / R) is the foundation of circuit analysis. In a series circuit, the total resistance is the sum of all individual resistances.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to add all resistances first — you must find R_total before dividing.
Dividing resistance by voltage instead of voltage by resistance.

prompt: Three resistors are connected in series: 22 Ω, 33 Ω, and 45 Ω. What is the total resistance in ohms?
hint: In a series circuit, total resistance is the sum of all individual resistances.

steps.title: Identify the circuit type
steps.body: The resistors are connected in series — one after another in a single path.

steps.title: Interpret the result
steps.body: The combined resistance of the three components is 100 Ω. The power source 'sees' this as a single 100 Ω load.

solution.whyItWorks: In a series circuit, current must pass through every resistor. Each one adds resistance to the path, so the total is always the sum.

commonMistakes: Using the parallel formula (1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + …) instead of simple addition — that formula is only for parallel circuits.

prompt: A device operates at 12 V and draws 3 A of current. What is the power consumption in watts?
hint: Power equals voltage times current: P = V × I.

solution.summary: The power formula connects voltage and current to energy consumption.

steps.title: Choose the formula
steps.body: Power is the rate at which electrical energy is used. It's measured in watts.

steps.title: Interpret the result
steps.body: The device consumes 36 watts of electrical power. This is similar to a small incandescent light bulb.
steps.note: Remember: watts tell you how fast energy is being used, not how much total energy is stored.

solution.whyItWorks: P = V × I works because power is the product of the 'push' (voltage) and the 'flow' (current). More push or more flow means more power.

commonMistakes: Confusing watts with watt-hours — watts measure instantaneous power, watt-hours measure energy over time.
Dividing voltage by current instead of multiplying.
